lists.title: Yoruba A1 Grammar
lists.description: Foundations: pronouns, tone, the copulas, location, negation, and the progressive.

lists.title: Yoruba A2 Grammar
lists.description: Aspect (perfect, future), objects, quality verbs, demonstratives, commands.

lists.title: Yoruba B1 Grammar
lists.description: Relatives, ni-focus, serial verbs, conditionals, comparison.

points.title: Subject pronouns (mo, o, ó…)
points.explanation: The short subject pronouns are mo (I), o (you), ó (he/she/it), a (we), ẹ (you all/polite), wọ́n (they). They sit right before the verb: Mo lọ (I went/go), Ó wá (He came). Yoruba verbs never change form — the pronoun and small particles do all the work.
points.culture_note: Ẹ and wọ́n double as respectful singulars — using the plural for an elder is basic politeness.

points.title: Tones change meaning
points.function: Hear and write the three tones
points.explanation: Yoruba has three tones: high (á), mid (a, unmarked), low (à) — and they distinguish words: ọkọ́ (hoe), ọkọ (husband), ọkọ̀ (vehicle). Every syllable carries a tone; writing the marks is part of spelling, not decoration.
points.culture_note: Talking drums imitate tone patterns — a drummer can 'speak' a proverb because tone carries that much meaning.

points.title: The copulas ni and jẹ́
points.explanation: Identity uses ni after the noun phrase: Olùkọ́ ni mí (I am a teacher — lit. teacher is me), Ta ni é? (Who are you?). Jẹ́ states category or profession before the noun: Mo jẹ́ olùkọ́ (I am a teacher). Both are A1 essentials; ni also powers focus later.

drills.translation: I am a teacher (it is a teacher that I am).

points.title: Location with wà and ní
points.function: Say where people and things are
points.explanation: 'Be located' is wà, and places take the preposition ní (sí for motion): Ó wà ní ilé (He is at home), Mo wà ní ọjà (I am at the market). With motion verbs: Mo lọ sí ilé-ìwé (I went to school).

points.title: Negation with kò
points.explanation: Kò before the verb negates it: Kò lọ (He didn't go), Mi ò fẹ́ (I don't want — mo becomes mi and kò often shortens to ò after it). Negating wà uses sí: Kò sí ni ilé (He is not at home); 'there isn't' is kò sí.

points.title: Progressive ń
points.function: Say what is happening right now
points.explanation: The preverb ń (high tone, written with its own letter) makes the progressive: Mo ń lọ (I am going), Ó ń jẹun (She is eating), Wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ (They are working).

points.title: Plural with àwọn
points.explanation: Nouns don't change for number; put àwọn before the noun when plurality matters: ọmọ (child/children) → àwọn ọmọ (the children), àwọn olùkọ́ (the teachers). Numbers make àwọn unnecessary: ọmọ méjì (two children).

points.title: Questions with ṣé and kí ni
points.explanation: Ṣé in front of a statement makes a yes/no question: Ṣé o fẹ́ omi? (Do you want water?). Content questions use kí ni (what), ta ni (who), níbo (where): Kí ni orúkọ rẹ? (What is your name?), Níbo lo ń lọ? (Where are you going?).

points.title: Possession (mi, rẹ, rẹ̀ / ti)
points.explanation: Possessive pronouns follow the noun: ìwé mi (my book), ilé rẹ (your house), ọkọ̀ rẹ̀ (his/her vehicle), ilé wa (our house). Between two nouns, possession is just order (or ti): ilé bàbá (father's house).

points.title: Perfect ti
points.explanation: Ti before the verb marks completion: Mo ti jẹun (I have eaten), Ó ti lọ (She has already left), Wọ́n ti dé (They have arrived). With kò it means 'not yet': Kò tíì dé (He hasn't arrived yet).

points.title: Future yóò / máa
points.explanation: Two future markers: yóò (with full pronouns: Ó yóò lọ → usually yó lọ) and máa, the everyday one: Mo máa lọ (I will go), A máa rìn (We will walk). Negative future uses kò ní í: Kò ní í lọ (He won't go).

points.title: Object pronouns
points.explanation: Object pronouns follow the verb and copy its final vowel's tone pattern: rí mi (see me), rí ẹ (see you), rí i (see him/her/it — vowel echo), rí wa (see us), rí yín (see you all), rí wọn (see them). Mo rí i (I saw him).

drills.translation: I saw him/her at the market.

points.title: Adjectives are verbs (dára, tóbi)
points.explanation: Most quality words are stative VERBS: Ó dára (It is good), Ilé tóbi (The house is big), Ó gùn (It is long) — no copula needed. Before a noun they become participles or use relative tí: ilé ńlá (a big house), ilé tí ó dára (a house that is good).

drills.hint: quality word used as a verb
quality word used as a verb

points.title: Demonstratives yìí and yẹn
points.function: Point out this one and that one
points.explanation: Yìí (this) and yẹn (that) follow the noun: ìwé yìí (this book), ọkùnrin yẹn (that man). Plural combines with àwọn: àwọn ìwé yìí (these books). Náà after a noun means 'the (aforementioned)': ilé náà (the house in question).

points.title: Imperatives and má (don't)
points.function: Tell people to do and not to do
points.explanation: Commands are the bare verb: Wá! (Come!), Jókòó! (Sit down!). Politeness adds jọ̀wọ́ (please) or the plural ẹ: Ẹ jọ̀wọ́, ẹ wá (Please come). The negative command is má (or má ṣe): Má lọ! (Don't go!).

points.title: Relative clauses with tí
points.explanation: Tí introduces relative clauses: ọkùnrin tí ó wá (the man who came), ìwé tí mo rà (the book that I bought), oúnjẹ tí wọ́n sè (the food they cooked). Inside the clause the normal pronouns stay.

drills.translation: The man who came is a teacher.
The book that I bought is good.

points.title: Focus with ni
points.function: Emphasize one part of the sentence
points.explanation: Moving a phrase to the front and adding ni focuses it: Adé ni ó lọ (It was Adé who went), Ọjà ni mo lọ (It's the market I went to), Àná ni wọ́n dé (It was yesterday they arrived). Question words use the same frame: Ta ni ó wá? (Who came?).
points.culture_note: This ni-focus is everywhere in real speech — answers to questions almost always use it.

drills.hint: focus marker after the fronted word

drills.translation: It is the market that I went to.

points.title: Serial verbs
points.function: Chain verbs to express one complex action
points.explanation: Yoruba stacks verbs without 'and': mú ìwé wá (take book come = bring the book), gbé e lọ (carry it go = take it away), ra oúnjẹ fún mi (buy food give me = buy food for me). Each verb keeps its own meaning; together they map one event.

points.title: Conditionals with bí … bá
points.explanation: Real conditions use bí (or tí) … bá: Bí o bá kàwé, wàá yege (If you study, you will pass), Bí òjò bá rọ̀, a ó dúró ní ilé (If it rains, we will stay home). Bá sits right before the verb of the if-clause.

drills.translation: If you study, you will pass.

drills.sentence: Bí òjò {{answer}} rọ̀, a ó dúró.
drills.translation: If it rains, we will stay.

points.title: Comparatives with ju … lọ
points.explanation: Comparison wraps ju … lọ around the standard: Ó ga ju mí lọ (He is taller than me), Ilé yìí tóbi ju ilé yẹn lọ (This house is bigger than that one). The quality word itself never changes.

drills.sentence: Ilé yìí tóbi ju ilé yẹn {{answer}}.
drills.translation: This house is bigger than that house.

points.title: Habitual máa ń
points.explanation: Máa ń (often written maa n) marks habits: Mo máa ń jí ní aago mẹ́fà (I usually wake at six), Wọ́n máa ń lọ sí ṣọ́ọ̀ṣì (They usually go to church). Negative habitual is kì í: Kì í mu ọtí (He doesn't (habitually) drink).

drills.sentence: Mo {{answer}} ń jí ní aago mẹ́fà.
drills.translation: I usually wake up at six o'clock.

points.title: Reported speech with pé
points.function: Report what people said and think
points.explanation: Pé introduces reported clauses after saying/thinking verbs: Ó sọ pé òun máa wá (He said that he would come), Mo mọ̀ pé o ti dé (I know that you have arrived). Note òun — the special third-person pronoun used inside reports to refer back to the speaker.

drills.translation: He said that he would come.
I know that you have arrived.

points.title: Impersonal wọ́n (passive meaning)
points.function: Say what 'is done' without a doer
points.explanation: Yoruba has no passive; impersonal wọ́n (they) covers it: Wọ́n ti kọ́ ilé náà (The house has been built — lit. they have built it), Wọ́n ń pè ọ́ (You are being called). Context, not form, signals the passive reading.

drills.translation: The house has been built (lit. they have built it).
You are being called (lit. they are calling you).

points.title: Although: bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé
points.explanation: The concessive frame bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé … (even though …) sets up contrast: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé òjò rọ̀, a lọ (Even though it rained, we went). A shorter alternative is síbẹ̀ (nevertheless) linking two clauses.

drills.sentence: Bí ó {{answer}} jẹ́ pé òjò rọ̀, a lọ.
drills.translation: Even though it rained, we went.
